questions.stem: Java interview: why is String immutable, and what's one practical consequence?
options: Thread-safety/sharing, hashCode caching, and safe use as HashMap keys; concatenating in a loop builds many objects
Immutability is only a style guide with no runtime effect
Immutable means you cannot read characters
It makes substring always O(1) forever in all JDKs
questions.explanation: Immutability enables sharing and safe hashing; naive + in loops is costly → StringBuilder.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Char vs code point — what do you say about emoji / UTF-16? Which response is most accurate?
options: Every Unicode character is exactly one 16-bit char in Java
UTF-16 may use surrogate pairs; length() counts chars, not always user-perceived characters
UTF-8 and UTF-16 are identical on disk
ASCII is required for all interview string problems
questions.explanation: Be aware of encoding: char length ≠ grapheme/code-point length.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Best structure to reverse a mutable character buffer in place? Which response is most accurate?
options: Two pointers swapping chars toward the center
questions.explanation: Same pattern as array reverse on a char array / StringBuilder.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Anagram check screening answer? Which response is most accurate?
options: Compare sorted versions, or count character frequencies
questions.explanation: Sort O(n log n) or count O(n+σ) — both are standard.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why prefer StringBuilder over repeated String + in a loop (Java)? Which response is most accurate?
options: Each + may allocate a new String; StringBuilder mutates a buffer amortized efficiently
StringBuilder is always slower but clearer
questions.explanation: Avoid quadratic allocation from immutable concatenation.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Palindrome check for a string — first approach you'd mention? Which response is most accurate?
options: Two pointers from ends comparing characters (optionally skipping non-alphanumeric)
questions.explanation: Two pointers is the screening default.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: What does RLE (run-length encoding) do at a high level? Which response is most accurate?
options: Replaces consecutive repeats with a count+value encoding (e.g. aabbb → a2b3)
questions.explanation: Compresses runs; interviewers ask encode/decode variants.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Case-insensitive compare — pitfall to mention? Which response is most accurate?
options: toLowerCase is locale-sensitive and can surprise (e.g. Turkish i)
questions.explanation: Locale and Unicode case folding are real footguns.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: null vs empty string in APIs — screening clarity? Which response is most accurate?
options: Empty is a valid zero-length string; null means absence — handle explicitly at boundaries
They are identical in all languages
null has length 0 safely in Java without checks
Empty strings cannot be HashMap keys
questions.explanation: Distinguish missing vs present-but-empty.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Substring search naive complexity you should state? Which response is most accurate?
options: O((n−m+1)·m) worst case for naive scan
O(log n) binary search on characters
questions.explanation: Naive can be quadratic; mention KMP/Rabin–Karp as upgrades.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Valid parentheses with multiple bracket types — structure? Which response is most accurate?
options: Stack: push openers, pop on matching closers
questions.explanation: Classic stack matching problem.

questions.stem: In a coding interview you build a frequency array for lowercase English letters only. What size should that array be, and why?
options: Size n — one slot per character in the input string
Size 2^32 — to cover every possible Unicode code point
Size log n — enough for binary encoding of counts
Size 26 (or 128/256 for ASCII) — fixed alphabet, independent of string length
questions.explanation: A fixed alphabet gives O(1) extra space relative to alphabet size; for a–z that is 26 buckets.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Trim spaces / normalize whitespace — interview expectation? Which response is most accurate?
options: Two pointers or StringBuilder skipping runs of spaces is fine and shows control
Regex only; manual two pointers are unacceptable
questions.explanation: Manual scan shows you understand indices and runs.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why can hashing a string be O(n)? Which response is most accurate?
options: You typically read all characters to compute the digest/hash
Hashing is always O(1) including long strings
questions.explanation: Content length dominates hash computation.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Immutable string as map key — why is that desirable? Which response is most accurate?
options: Only mutable keys are allowed in Java HashMap
Hash stays stable; mutation would corrupt bucket placement
questions.explanation: Stable hashCode/equals over the key's lifetime.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: First unique character in a stream/string — basic tool? Which response is most accurate?
options: Frequency count (array/map) then second pass for first count==1
questions.explanation: Count then scan for first unique.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Byte string vs Unicode string — why interviewers ask? Which response is most accurate?
options: Encoding boundaries (UTF-8 bytes vs characters) affect length, slicing, and I/O
They are the same in every system
questions.explanation: Clarify whether the problem is about bytes or characters.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Check if one string is rotation of another — slick test? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Classic doubling trick plus substring search.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Character frequency map vs sorted string for anagrams — tradeoff? Which response is most accurate?
options: Sorting always uses less memory than a 26-count
Counting is O(n) time with O(σ) space; sorting is O(n log n) and simple
questions.explanation: State both; pick based on alphabet and constraints.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Screening: access i-th character of a contiguous string/char array? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Same as array indexing for typical string representations.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Longest substring without repeating characters — pattern? Which response is most accurate?
options: Sliding window + last-seen index / set of chars in window
questions.explanation: Shrink left when a duplicate enters; track max window length.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Minimum window substring containing all chars of t — approach? Which response is most accurate?
options: Sliding window with need/have counters; shrink when window is valid
Binary search each character of t independently
questions.explanation: Classic hard sliding-window with frequency deficits.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Group anagrams — map key you propose? Which response is most accurate?
options: Sorted char signature or 26-count tuple as key → list of words
questions.explanation: Canonical key buckets anagrams together.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Longest palindromic substring — expandable approach? Which response is most accurate?
options: Only DP n² table is allowed
Two pointers from ends of the whole string only once
Expand around centers (2n−1 centers) for O(n²) time O(1) space
questions.explanation: Center expansion is a common interview solution; Manacher is advanced.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Edit distance (Levenshtein) — DP meaning of dp[i][j]? Which response is most accurate?
options: Min edits to transform first i chars of s into first j of t
questions.explanation: Standard definition for insert/delete/replace DP.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: KMP at high level — what precomputation helps? Which response is most accurate?
options: Longest proper prefix that is also suffix (LPS/π) for the pattern to avoid re-checking
Bloom filter of all pattern rotations
questions.explanation: Failure function lets the search continue without full backup.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Word break (dictionary) — typical technique? Which response is most accurate?
options: Greedy take longest word always correct
DP/BFS: reachable split positions given dict membership
questions.explanation: Reachability over prefixes with dictionary lookups.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Decode ways ('12' → AB or L) — pitfall? Which response is most accurate?
options: Leading zeros are always valid decodings
Handle '0' carefully; invalid singles/pairs; DP on prior one/two digit validity
questions.explanation: Zeros and valid 10–26 pairs make naive recursion wrong.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Compare version numbers '1.01' vs '1.001' — approach? Which response is most accurate?
options: Split on '.', parse segments as integers (or bigints), compare pairwise
questions.explanation: Numeric segment compare, not raw lexicographic.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Simplify Unix path — tool? Which response is most accurate?
options: Stack of path parts: push dirs, pop on '..', ignore '.' and empties
questions.explanation: Stack models directory navigation.

questions.stem: String compression in-place (LeetCode-style) with counts — what do you watch?
options: Multi-digit counts, write pointer, and not overflowing read ahead incorrectly
questions.explanation: Digit length of counts and two-pointer write are the hard parts.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Is subsequence (s in t) — clean check? Which response is most accurate?
options: Two pointers: advance in t, advance in s on matches
questions.explanation: Greedy scan preserves order without contiguity.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Longest common prefix of string array — strategies? Which response is most accurate?
options: Vertical scan / horizontal shrink / sort and compare first-last
questions.explanation: Several O(total length) approaches; sorting trick is neat.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Rabin–Karp idea in one line? Which response is most accurate?
options: Build KMP π on the text
Rolling hash of windows equals pattern hash, then verify to dodge collisions
questions.explanation: Rolling hash enables O(n+m) expected search with verification.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Palindrome via removing at most one char — technique? Which response is most accurate?
options: Two pointers; on mismatch try skip left or skip right once
questions.explanation: Greedy recover with one deletion attempt.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Multiply two big integers given as strings — outline? Which response is most accurate?
options: Grade-school multiply into an int array of size n+m, then convert to string
questions.explanation: Simulate multiplication; watch carry and leading zeros.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Find all anagrams of p in s — pattern? Which response is most accurate?
options: Fixed sliding window of len(p) with frequency match counters
Trie of all permutations of p (n!)
questions.explanation: Window frequency equality is the mid-level standard.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Z-algorithm high-level purpose? Which response is most accurate?
options: For each i, longest substring starting at i that matches a prefix — aids pattern matching
Build a balanced BST of chars
questions.explanation: Z-box / Z-array is another linear pattern tool.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Interleaving string DP — question shape? Which response is most accurate?
options: Whether s3 is an anagram of s1+s2 only
Whether s1 is rotation of s2
Whether s3 merges s1 and s2 preserving order
questions.explanation: Order-preserving merge; DP on prefixes of s1/s2.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Count-and-say sequence — how do you generate the next term? Which response is most accurate?
options: Apply RLE to the previous term's digit runs
questions.explanation: Next = run-length description of current.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Senior Java: String.substring historically shared backing array — risk? Which answer is best?
options: Retaining a tiny substring could pin a huge backing char[] (memory leak pattern); newer JDKs copy
questions.explanation: Know historical sharing vs copy-on-substring behavior.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Rolling hash pitfalls in interviews? Which response is most accurate?
options: Mod overflow, base choice, and false positives — always verify on hash match
questions.explanation: Probabilistic match needs confirmation; watch modular arithmetic.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: UTF-8 validation / slicing mid-sequence — why dangerous? Which response is most accurate?
options: UTF-8 is fixed 4 bytes per char always
Cutting inside a multi-byte code point yields invalid sequences / wrong characters
Validation is unnecessary for network I/O
questions.explanation: Respect code-point boundaries when slicing bytes.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: When is KMP overkill vs naive in an interview? Which response is most accurate?
options: Small n·m or one-off search: naive/clarified library search may be enough; discuss complexity
questions.explanation: Match algorithm to constraints; show you know when quadratic is fine.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Interning strings — tradeoff? Which response is most accurate?
options: Saves memory on duplicates / speeds == sometimes, but uses a global pool and can increase GC/perm pressure
questions.explanation: Pool benefits vs lifetime and contention costs.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Surrogate pairs and charAt loops — bug pattern? Which response is most accurate?
options: charAt always returns full code points
Iterating by char can split emoji; use codePointAt / offsetByCodePoints when required
questions.explanation: UTF-16 indexing ≠ user-perceived characters.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Rope / gap buffer vs flat string for editors — why? Which response is most accurate?
options: Flat arrays always win for insert-middle
Split/concat-friendly structures make middle edits cheaper than O(n) array shifts
questions.explanation: Editable text needs structures beyond immutable flat strings.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Security: using user strings as format patterns / SQL — lesson? Which response is most accurate?
options: Never treat untrusted input as format/query structure; use parameterization and safe APIs
questions.explanation: Injection is about interpretation, not mutability.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Comparing large strings for equality — micro tradeoff? Which response is most accurate?
options: Early exit on first mismatch; hash equality can short-circuit but collisions still need equals
Always compute full hash first then skip equals
== on contents works in Java without equals
questions.explanation: Cheap filters first; equals remains source of truth.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Manacher's algorithm — when do you mention it? Which response is most accurate?
options: As the only way to check a palindrome
When you need all longest palindromic substrings in linear time; otherwise center expand may suffice
It replaces KMP for pattern search
It only works on even-length strings
questions.explanation: Know it exists; justify when O(n) palindromic radius is needed.
